text: This chapter focuses on the development of the relationship between the central and local government in Japan, particularly in relation to the administration, finance and politics, and explains in what way their intergovernmental system is characterized by the interpenetration and differentiation of the central and local state bureaucracy. Japan adopted the European system of bureaucracy and therefore the interfusion model at the time of its modernization. This relationship has developed in various ways throughout the 20th century, such as wartime centralization, the post-1945 decentralization policy implemented by the Occupation forces, and the 1990s reform of decentralization. So, in spite of all these changes, the interfusion between the central and local state bureaucracy has not only been maintained but also reinforced. This is due to a number of structural factors, including the nationwide amalgamation of municipalities, and the sophisticated methods of fiscal harmonization. This means that local government is carrying out a very high proportion of public activities, a figure which is even higher than that of many other OECD countries, and which is therefore fully based on the interpenetration of the local state bureaucracy and the central state in an interfusion type of relationship.
explanation: The passage is coherent, domain-specific, and uses formal academic phrasing, which is typical of human-authored scholarly text; however, the dense synthesis of historical periods and repetition of specialized terms could also be produced by AI.
